Seqrite detailed Operation Dragon Weave, a China-aligned campaign targeting government, research, academic, technology, and financial organizations in the Czech Republic and Taiwan using Rust-based tooling and Azure Blob Storage dead-drop C2. Other reporting pointed to a separate suspected China-linked intrusion using the Go-based TencShell implant, while ESET assessed China-aligned groups remained highly active globally into 2026. Check Point's roundup added that the Russia-aligned GREYVIBE group used ChatGPT and Google Gemini to support phishing, malware development, and post-compromise actions against Ukrainian targets. The same reporting referenced a destructive Iran-linked operation against LA Metro and a social-engineering-driven Carnival data breach affecting nearly 6 million people. On the disruption and governance side, Microsoft investigated separate outages affecting Office for the web and Teams file access, as well as MFA setup and the My Sign-Ins platform, with the latter tied to a cache configuration change and failover-related resource pressure. Elsewhere, Spanish police arrested a suspect accused of leaking sensitive personal data tied to multiple state institutions, and the NSA announced new leadership for its Cybersecurity Directorate and Collaboration Center amid broader organizational turbulence.

Critical Windows Netlogon RCE flaw now exploited in attacks — Must Read

Belgium’s national cybersecurity authority warns that attackers are now actively exploiting CVE-2026-41089, a critical Windows Netlogon remote code execution flaw affecting domain controllers. Organizations should urgently patch supported Windows Server systems, including Windows Server 2025, to reduce the risk of compromise.

  • The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium warned that CVE-2026-41089, a critical Windows Netlogon flaw, is now being actively exploited in the wild.
  • Microsoft patched CVE-2026-41089 during the May 2026 Patch Tuesday and described it as a stack-based buffer overflow that can allow remote code execution on domain controllers without authentication.
  • The vulnerability affects all currently supported Windows Server versions, including Windows Server 2025.
  • The Belgian cybersecurity authority urged administrators to patch vulnerable servers immediately, but it did not provide additional details about the observed attacks.
  • Microsoft had not updated its advisory to confirm active exploitation at the time of the report, and the company did not reply to BleepingComputer’s request for confirmation.

Critical WP Maps Pro Flaw Actively Exploited to Create Admin Accounts — Must Read

Attackers are actively exploiting a critical WP Maps Pro flaw, CVE-2026-8732, to create administrator accounts and fully take over vulnerable WordPress sites. Any site running version 6.1.0 or earlier should update immediately to 6.1.1.

  • Threat actors are actively attempting to exploit a critical WP Maps Pro vulnerability to create malicious administrator accounts on vulnerable WordPress sites.
  • The flaw, tracked as CVE-2026-8732 with a CVSS score of 9.8, affects all WP Maps Pro versions up to and including 6.1.0 and was fixed in version 6.1.1.
  • According to Wordfence, the vulnerability stems from a temporary access feature that lets unauthenticated users invoke the wpgmp_temp_access_support() function and create a new WordPress administrator account.
  • The plugin maintainers released a patch on May 20, 2026, that restricts access to the vulnerable endpoint to authenticated administrators only.
  • Wordfence said it blocked 2,858 attacks targeting the issue over the past 24 hours, and urged site owners to update to the latest version.

Miasma Supply Chain Attack Compromises Red Hat npm Packages with Credential-Stealing Worm — Should Read

Several @redhat-cloud-services npm packages were compromised in a supply chain campaign called Miasma that steals developer and CI/CD secrets, exfiltrates them in encrypted form, and can spread by abusing stolen GitHub and npm credentials. Organizations that installed the affected versions should treat hosts and build artifacts as potentially compromised, rotate credentials, and check for persistence beyond simply removing the packages.

  • A supply chain campaign dubbed Miasma compromised multiple @redhat-cloud-services npm packages with a malicious preinstall hook that steals credentials and secrets from developer machines and CI/CD environments.
  • Researchers said the malware collects GitHub Actions secrets, npm tokens, cloud credentials, Kubernetes and Vault material, SSH keys, Git credentials, and other sensitive files, then exfiltrates them using encrypted logic to api.anthropic.com with GitHub as a fallback.
  • The malware can enumerate GitHub repositories writable by a stolen token, read workflow files, and commit malicious workflows through the GitHub API to support downstream propagation.
  • The article states the malware also attempts privilege escalation in CI, checks for protections such as CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Carbon Black, and StepSecurity Harden-Runner, and establishes persistence through Anthropic Claude Code and Visual Studio Code configuration changes.
  • Evidence cited in the article suggests a compromised Red Hat employee GitHub account was the initial access point used to inject malicious orphan commits into two RedHatInsights repositories, bypassing code review.

OpenAI Codex Authentication Tokens Stolen in codexui-android npm Supply Chain Attack — Should Read

A popular npm package, codexui-android, and related Android apps were found stealing OpenAI Codex authentication tokens by secretly exfiltrating the local auth.json file to an attacker-controlled server. The key takeaway is that developers using this tool may have exposed long-lived refresh tokens that can enable persistent account access.

  • Aikido Security reported that the npm package codexui-android, promoted as a remote web UI for OpenAI Codex, has been exfiltrating Codex authentication tokens to the attacker-controlled domain sentry.anyclaw.store.
  • The malicious code reads the plaintext file ~/.codex/auth.json and sends access_token, refresh_token, id_token, and account ID data to the remote server.
  • Aikido said the exfiltration was added about a month after the package was first published, and the package's GitHub repository remained clean while the npm build contained the malicious functionality.
  • The same exfiltration chain was also found in Android apps including OpenClaw Codex Claude AI Agent and Codex, which run the npm package inside a PRoot sandbox and send credentials after users sign in.
  • The article also notes separate Aikido findings that deleted Google API keys can remain usable for up to 23 minutes, and that Google has since decided to treat the issue as a P0 bug.

WordPress malware campaign hides payloads in Steam profiles — Should Read

Hackers infected nearly 2,000 WordPress sites with malware that hid its command-and-control payload inside Steam Community profile comments using invisible Unicode characters. The campaign let attackers quietly load malicious JavaScript and maintain a PHP backdoor while blending in with normal WordPress and Steam traffic.

  • GoDaddy researchers found malware on about 1,980 WordPress websites in a campaign that used Steam Community profile comments to hide command-and-control data.
  • The malware encoded payloads with six invisible Unicode characters embedded in benign-looking Steam comments, allowing visible text to act as camouflage.
  • The decoded payload built a URL to hello-mywordl.info, which served JavaScript disguised as legitimate library files and injected it into every frontend WordPress page.
  • The final stage installed a PHP backdoor that accepted base64-encoded code through POST requests when a specific authentication cookie was present.
  • GoDaddy said defenders should look for references to Steam Community URLs, suspicious external JavaScript injections, invisible Unicode characters, and requests involving hello-mywordl.info or the malware’s authentication cookies.

Microsoft fixes KB5089549 Windows security update install issues — Should Read

Microsoft fixed the Windows 11 KB5089549 install failures caused by low EFI System Partition space in the KB5089573 preview update, with the broader fix rolling out in June Patch Tuesday updates. Affected users can also use Microsoft's workaround or Known Issue Rollback if they haven’t installed the newer update yet.

  • Microsoft resolved a known issue that caused installation failures and 0x800f0922 errors when deploying the May 2026 Windows 11 security update KB5089549.
  • Microsoft said the failures were caused by insufficient free space on the EFI System Partition, especially on devices with 10 MB or less available, and the update could fail during reboot at around 35 to 36 percent completion.
  • The issue was fixed in the Windows 11 KB5089573 preview cumulative update released on May 26, 2026, and the fix will also be included in the June Patch Tuesday updates.
  • Users who do not install the optional May 2026 update can mitigate the problem with Known Issue Rollback, and enterprise admins can deploy a Group Policy workaround provided by Microsoft.
  • Microsoft noted that affected users may see a "Something didn't go as planned. Undoing changes." message and log entries such as "SpaceCheck" and "ServicingBootFiles failed" when the update rolls back.

Hackers hijack thousands of sites for ClickFix and FakeUpdate attacks — Should Read

DriveSurge compromised thousands of legitimate websites and used the zTDS traffic system to redirect visitors into ClickFix and fake browser update scams that install malware on Windows and macOS. The key takeaway is that users should only update browsers through official in-app settings and never run copied terminal or PowerShell commands from web prompts.

  • Silent Push says the threat actor DriveSurge compromised thousands of legitimate websites and redirected visitors to malware delivery infrastructure.
  • The campaign used the zTDS traffic distribution system to profile visitors and decide whether to show a ClickFix lure or a FakeUpdates prompt.
  • Silent Push described DriveSurge as primarily functioning as an initial access broker operating on a pay-per-install model.
  • The fake update lures impersonated browser updates for products including Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari, Opera, Brave, Yandex, Vivaldi, Samsung Internet, and UC Browser.
  • Researchers also found an obfuscated JavaScript payload targeting macOS systems through verification-themed ClickFix attacks that hijack the clipboard.

Spain arrests doxer leaking sensitive data of govt employees — Should Read

Spanish police arrested a suspected doxer accused of leaking sensitive personal data tied to key government and security institutions, including INCIBE, creating national security concerns. Authorities say the data appears to have been aggregated from existing sources rather than stolen directly from INCIBE systems, and more arrests may follow.

  • The Spanish National Police arrested an individual accused of leaking sensitive personal data linked to key state organizations, including INCIBE, the State Attorney General's Office, the National Police, the Civil Guard, and the National Security Council.
  • Police said the mass dissemination of the data created an immediate risk to the security and integrity of both the affected individuals and the institutions involved.
  • Authorities raided the suspect’s home and seized computers and other electronic devices for forensic examination.
  • INCIBE said in February that it had not been directly compromised and that the operation involved the targeted collection and publication of data affecting key entities and their employees.
  • The threat group reportedly associated with the leak was Police-ESP-Doxed, and police are examining seized devices for evidence of additional participants.

Inspector general finds NIST mistakes have made vulnerability database ineffective — Should Read

A Commerce Department watchdog found that NIST’s mismanagement has made the National Vulnerability Database increasingly ineffective, with its backlog of unprocessed flaws growing past 27,000 and duplicate work with CISA wasting resources. The report says NIST needs major process changes, better coordination, and less time spent on low-value severity scoring to restore the database’s usefulness and trust.

  • A Department of Commerce inspector general report found that NIST’s National Vulnerability Database backlog grew from 13,000 unprocessed vulnerabilities in February 2024 to more than 27,000 by the end of 2025, undermining its utility and public trust.
  • The report says poor planning by NIST contributed to the backlog, including a failed pledge to process about 6,200 vulnerabilities per month despite historically never exceeding 5,000 and lacking a plan to meet the target.
  • The inspector general found that NIST and CISA duplicated work in at least 21,000 instances between May 2024 and December 2025, wasting about $200,000 because NIST did not coordinate with CISA’s Vulnrichment program.
  • The report recommends that NIST spend less time assigning severity scores and identifying impacted products, noting that 80% of submissions already include severity scores and that NIST’s scores matched independent assessors only 12% of the time.
  • NIST concurred with the recommendations and said it would begin improving operations immediately, while Cyber Threat Alliance CEO Michael Daniel argued that responsibility for the NVD should move to CISA.

Dashlane password manager users locked out by brute force attacks — Should Read

Dashlane says some user accounts were temporarily suspended after an external brute-force attack triggered its automated protections, and it claims there is no evidence its systems were compromised. Although Dashlane marked the incident resolved and said affected accounts were unsuspended, some users still report login issues.

  • Dashlane confirmed that certain user accounts were targeted in a brute-force attack by an external party, which led to account suspensions through its built-in security controls.
  • The company said there is no evidence of compromise of Dashlane’s systems and that the affected accounts have now been unsuspended.
  • Users reported receiving emails about suspicious access requests from foreign countries that included verification codes for registering new devices.
  • Dashlane’s status page shows the company opened an investigation on May 31 at 15:19 UTC and marked the issue resolved by 22:30 UTC.
  • Despite Dashlane marking the incident as resolved, some users continued to report login problems and said support was unresponsive.

Microsoft fixes outage affecting MFA setup, MySignIn service — Should Read

Microsoft restored the My Sign-Ins service after an outage that blocked some users from setting up MFA or accessing mysignins.microsoft.com, often with 504 errors. The company said a recent cache configuration change triggered a failover that led to high CPU and memory usage during peak EU traffic.

  • Microsoft investigated and then mitigated an incident that prevented some users from setting up multi-factor authentication or accessing the My Sign-Ins service at mysignins.microsoft.com.
  • Affected users encountered 504 Gateway Timeout errors when trying to access mysignins.microsoft.com, according to Microsoft’s admin center notice MO1329260.
  • Microsoft said it completed mitigation actions including failing over to alternate infrastructure and continued monitoring service telemetry and evaluating additional mitigation options as elevated error rates persisted.
  • In a later update, Microsoft said it restored access to the My Sign-Ins service and attributed the incident to a recent cache configuration change that required a failover.
  • Microsoft said the failover caused high CPU and memory utilization as EU traffic peaked, preventing the MySignIn service from processing the volume of requests, and that mitigation actions were rolled back with traffic restored to the original infrastructure.

Microsoft investigates Office Apps, Teams file access issues — Should Read

Microsoft investigated a cross-service incident that prevented some users from opening files in Office for the web and Microsoft Teams, affecting apps like Excel and PowerPoint. The company later said the outage had been resolved, though engineers are still investigating the root cause.

  • Microsoft said an incident was preventing some users from opening files in Office for the web and Microsoft Teams.
  • The issue affected multiple Office apps, including Excel and PowerPoint for the web, and displayed an error stating that Office Online services were not available.
  • Microsoft said initial analysis pointed to a potential cross-service issue impacting Office for the web experiences and that it was investigating service telemetry to isolate the root cause.
  • The company later said the issue had been resolved and that the service had remained stable for an extended period.
  • Microsoft also said the outage recovered without any specific engineering actions and that engineers were continuing to investigate the underlying cause to prevent recurrence.

China-Aligned Groups Ramp Up Attacks: Dragon Weave Hits Czech Republic & Taiwan — Should Read

A China-aligned espionage campaign called Operation Dragon Weave is targeting victims in the Czech Republic and Taiwan with spear-phishing emails that deploy the AdaptixC2 malware through a stealthy Azure-based command-and-control setup. The report also highlights broader ongoing activity by multiple China-linked groups using tools like TencShell, PhiliKit, and Cobalt Strike against government, technology, and manufacturing targets worldwide.

  • Seqrite Labs identified Operation Dragon Weave, a China-aligned cyber espionage campaign targeting officials and citizens in the Czech Republic and Taiwan across government, research, academic, technology, and financial services sectors.
  • The campaign uses spear-phishing emails with ZIP attachments that trigger an infection chain involving a malicious LNK file or a Rust-based dropper, DLL side-loading of UnityPlayer.dll, and deployment of the Rust-based loader RUSTCLOAK.
  • RUSTCLOAK decrypts and launches the AdaptixC2 payload called AZUREVEIL, which uses Microsoft Azure Blob Storage as a dead-drop command-and-control channel and supports 36 commands for file operations, shell execution, process control, port forwarding, SOCKS proxy control, and in-memory BOF execution.
  • Cato Networks reported blocking a separate suspected China-linked intrusion against the Indian branch of a global manufacturer that attempted to deploy TencShell, a previously undocumented Go-based implant derived from the open-source rshell C2 framework.
  • ESET said China-aligned threat actors remained highly active globally from October 2025 through March 2026, and it linked groups and tools including SteppeDriver, PhiliKit, NegativeGlimmer, AdaptixC2, and Cobalt Strike to activity in countries such as France, Mongolia, Panama, Cambodia, and South Korea.

Microsoft says it will not pursue security researchers after zero-day backlash — Should Read

Microsoft said it does not plan to take legal action against security researchers for publishing their findings after backlash over language seen as threatening in a recent zero-day blog post. The company also acknowledged some researcher interactions have fallen short, while a researcher at the center of the dispute says more Windows flaws are coming.

  • Microsoft said it has no intention to pursue action against individuals conducting or publishing security research after backlash over an earlier blog post.
  • The earlier Microsoft post called recent uncoordinated Windows zero-day releases "never justifiable" and said the company’s Digital Crimes Unit would continue bringing cases against those enabling criminal actors.
  • Microsoft acknowledged that some interactions with researchers have fallen short and said it is working to learn from those incidents, but it did not directly address Nightmare Eclipse’s specific allegations.
  • The new Microsoft statement used the term "Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure" instead of "responsible disclosure," which had appeared repeatedly in the original post.
  • Nightmare Eclipse said on their blog that other researchers had approached them following recent events and announced a new Secure Boot vulnerability would be released sometime in June.

Race Against Time: Why Faster Vulnerability Alerts Matter — May Read

The article argues that organizations need near-real-time vulnerability alerts because attackers are exploiting newly disclosed flaws much faster than before, often before delayed feeds like the NVD are updated. It promotes SecAlerts as a way to match software inventories to immediate, filtered vulnerability notifications so teams can patch sooner and reduce breach risk.

  • The article says new vulnerabilities increased by 67% between 2023 and 2025, while exploited vulnerabilities rose by around 30% over the same period.
  • It states that the median time from CVE disclosure to exploitation has dropped to 1.6 days, down from 4.2 months in 2023.
  • The article argues that businesses often struggle to track the large amount of software they use, which can lead to missed alerts, delayed patches, or overlooked notifications.
  • It says services that rely solely on the NVD can face delays and notes that NIST has stopped prioritizing lower-priority vulnerabilities because of the scale of CVE growth.
  • The article promotes SecAlerts as a service that matches vulnerability alerts to an organization’s software and can deliver notifications by email, Slack, Teams, Jira, or Webhook.

NSA selects new leads for key cybersecurity posts — Optional

The NSA has chosen David Imbordino and Holly Baroody to permanently lead its Cybersecurity Directorate and Bruce Jones to head its Cybersecurity Collaboration Center, signaling a push to stabilize key cyber leadership roles after a turbulent year. The appointments come as the agency refocuses on rebuilding operations and expanding its work with industry and AI security.

  • The NSA selected David Imbordino as the new chief of its Cybersecurity Directorate and Holly Baroody as his deputy after both had previously served in acting roles.
  • The agency also chose Bruce Jones to lead the Cybersecurity Collaboration Center, an unclassified hub where government and private sector experts share information about hacking threats.
  • These appointments come after a turbulent period at the NSA that included a long leadership vacuum, high-level departures, and thousands of career personnel leaving amid workforce reduction pressure from the Trump administration.
  • The article says the NSA is working to determine how to incorporate artificial intelligence into its operations and define its role in the federal government on the technology.
  • Darren Turner said the Cybersecurity Collaboration Center and the Artificial Intelligence Security Center are central to the NSA’s efforts, and he noted that the collaboration center works with 1,900 private sector entities.

Containers on fire: from container escapes to supply chain attacks — Optional

Container attacks increasingly focus on misconfigurations, exposed orchestration APIs, and poisoned supply chains to steal secrets, escape to the host, and take over Kubernetes environments. The key takeaway is that container security depends on hardening the entire stack—from runtime privileges and host isolation to CI/CD pipelines and image trust—not just the container itself.

  • The article identifies major container attack vectors as host and runtime vulnerability exploitation, malicious activity inside compromised containers, container escape, orchestration API abuse, and supply chain compromise.
  • It highlights runC and Linux kernel flaws including CVE-2019-5736, CVE-2022-0492, and CVE-2024-21626 as examples of vulnerabilities that can enable host file system access, arbitrary code execution, or container escape.
  • The article explains that misconfigurations such as running privileged containers or granting capabilities like CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_MODULE, CAP_SYS_PTRACE, and CAP_NET_ADMIN can severely weaken isolation and facilitate host compromise.
  • It states that exposed or overprivileged Docker, Kubernetes, and kubelet APIs can allow attackers to create privileged containers, execute commands, mount host file systems, and ultimately take over hosts or clusters.
  • The article says supply chain attacks increasingly target public container images and CI/CD pipelines, allowing attackers to inject malicious code before containers are deployed into trusted environments.

1st June – Threat Intelligence Report — Optional

This weekly threat intelligence roundup highlights major data breaches, active exploitation of high-risk flaws, and the growing use of AI by threat actors to speed up phishing, malware development, and fraud. The key takeaway is that organizations face simultaneous pressure from credential-driven intrusions, rapidly weaponized vulnerabilities, and increasingly sophisticated AI-enabled campaigns.

  • Carnival Corporation confirmed a data breach affecting nearly 6 million people after attackers used social engineering to compromise an employee account.
  • Researchers reported that the Russia-aligned GREYVIBE group used ChatGPT and Google Gemini to support phishing, malware development, and post-compromise activity against Ukrainian targets.
  • CVE-2026-0257, a PAN-OS GlobalProtect authentication bypass fixed earlier in the month, is now being exploited against unpatched Palo Alto Networks devices and was added to CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 29.
  • A critical remote code execution vulnerability in Gogs with a CVSS score of 9.4 has no patch available, and Check Point IPS provides protection against the threat.
  • Researchers attributed a destructive campaign against LA Metro to an Iran-linked intelligence operation using the Ababil of Minab persona.

⚡ Weekly Recap: New Linux Flaw, PAN-OS Exploit, AI-Powered Attacks, OAuth Phishing and More — Optional

This week’s biggest takeaway is that attackers are moving faster than ever, with active exploitation hitting widely used products like Palo Alto PAN-OS GlobalProtect and multiple software supply-chain and phishing campaigns abusing AI to scale attacks. Organizations should urgently patch internet-facing systems, especially known exploited flaws, and watch for low-cost attacker paths like poisoned developer tools, OAuth phishing, and remote access malware.

  • Palo Alto Networks said CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass affecting PAN-OS and Prisma Access with specific GlobalProtect configurations, is being actively exploited in the wild.
  • Rapid7 reported a critical zero-day in the self-hosted Git service Gogs that can lead to remote code execution through malicious branch names in pull requests, and no patch was available at the time of publication.
  • CrowdStrike, Google, and the Shadowserver Foundation simultaneously took down all four command-and-control channels used by the GlassWorm malware operation, which had spread through trojanized VS Code extensions and compromised npm and Python packages.
  • CERT-In urged organizations in India to patch actively exploited vulnerabilities on internet-facing or crown-jewel systems within 12 hours where feasible because AI is compressing the time between disclosure and exploitation.
  • The article highlights several AI-enabled threats, including GREYVIBE’s operational use of large language models in attacks against Ukraine and phishing campaigns that abuse OAuth device code flows and chatbot search results to deliver malware or gain remote access.

Webinar tomorrow: From alert to resolution in network incident response — Optional

BleepingComputer and Tines are hosting a live webinar on June 2, 2026 about using automation and AI-assisted workflows to speed up network incident response from alert to resolution. The session will focus on reducing manual investigation delays, enriching alerts with context, and improving coordination across systems.

  • BleepingComputer will host a live webinar with Tines on June 2, 2026 titled "From alert to resolution: Fixing the gaps in network incident response."
  • The webinar will examine how IT teams can use automation and AI-assisted workflows to reduce investigation delays, improve coordination, and accelerate incident resolution.
  • The article says network incident response is often slowed after the initial alert because teams must manually gather context, determine ownership, and coordinate actions across multiple systems.
  • According to the article, Tines helps organizations streamline operational workflows by connecting systems, automating repetitive tasks, and moving incidents from detection to resolution more efficiently.
  • The webinar will cover alert enrichment with network, identity, and threat context, incident prioritization and routing, and coordinated resolution across systems.

The Security Growth Platform: Why MSPs Are Moving Beyond vCISO Tools — Optional

The article argues that MSPs have outgrown traditional vCISO and GRC tools and now need a broader “Security Growth Platform” that combines security management, compliance, multi-tenant operations, and revenue growth features in one system. Its main takeaway is that scaling a modern MSP security practice requires portfolio-level delivery and business intelligence, not just advisory tooling for individual engagements.

  • The article argues that MSPs and MSSPs need a new category called a Security Growth Platform because traditional vCISO tools and enterprise GRC platforms do not fully support portfolio-based security delivery.
  • It says enterprise compliance automation platforms were built for end customers with internal security teams and are not architected for service providers managing security programs across many SMB clients.
  • The article states that standalone vCISO tools focus on assessments, advisory frameworks, and reporting, but often lack the compliance depth and automation needed for ongoing multi-client security programs.
  • According to the article, a Security Growth Platform is defined by five capabilities: built-in CISO intelligence, unified security and compliance across 40+ frameworks, complete security lifecycle management, portfolio-level revenue intelligence, and MSP-scale multi-tenant architecture.
  • The article identifies Cynomi as the named example of this category and cites its reported partner outcomes, including reduced assessment workload, improved margins, revenue growth, and shorter discovery time.

item.title: WP Maps Pro CVE-2026-8732 Exploitation and Dutch Asocks Botnet Takedown
item.description: The reporting in this coverage window highlights two sharply different but operationally important security themes: rapid exploitation of exposed internet applications and large-scale disruption of criminal infrastructure. Together, the stories show both the speed at which newly disclosed web flaws...
item.content:encoded:
The reporting in this coverage window highlights two sharply different but operationally important security themes: rapid exploitation of exposed internet applications and large-scale disruption of criminal infrastructure. Together, the stories show both the speed at which newly disclosed web flaws can be weaponized and the scale of device abuse underpinning proxy-enabled cybercrime ecosystems. The most urgent defensive signal is active exploitation of a critical WordPress plugin vulnerability that enables full administrator compromise. In parallel, Dutch authorities announced a major botnet disruption tied to a proxy service reportedly backed by millions of infected devices across consumer, mobile, and IoT environments. Attackers are actively targeting WordPress sites running vulnerable versions of WP Maps Pro through CVE-2026-8732, a critical flaw affecting version 6.1.0 and earlier. The issue stems from a temporary access feature whose AJAX endpoint was reachable without authentication and depended on a nonce exposed in frontend JavaScript, undermining its intended access control. According to reporting from Wordfence, successful exploitation allows an attacker to create a new WordPress administrator account, generate a passwordless login URL, and automatically authenticate into the newly created admin session. That chain effectively turns a plugin-level bug into immediate full-site administrative takeover, making it especially relevant for threat hunters monitoring suspicious account creation, privilege escalation, and anomalous WordPress admin activity. Wordfence said it blocked more than 3,600 exploitation attempts in a 24-hour period, indicating broad and active abuse rather than isolated proof-of-concept scanning. The vendor released WP Maps Pro 6.1.1 on May 20 to address the flaw, but the reporting suggests a meaningful installed base remained exposed long enough to attract opportunistic attacker activity. Dutch authorities announced the dismantling of a botnet linked to at least 17 million infected devices, spanning computers, smartphones, tablets, and IoT systems. The Dutch Politie and National Cyber Security Center said more than 200 servers in the Netherlands functioned as backend infrastructure supporting the operation. Police reportedly seized part of the server estate from a hosting provider, after which the provider took the broader botnet infrastructure offline once its criminal use was identified. Local reporting cited by The Hacker News identified the disrupted service as Asocks, a company known for offering residential, mobile, and corporate proxy services. The case underscores the continuing overlap between botnet operations and commercialized proxy ecosystems, where compromised endpoints can be monetized as relay infrastructure. For defenders, the significance lies less in a single malware family and more in the persistence of large-scale device hijacking as a foundational service layer for anonymization, fraud enablement, and follow-on intrusion activity.

WP Maps Pro bug exploited to create admin accounts on WordPress sites — Must Read

Hackers are actively exploiting a critical flaw in WP Maps Pro versions 6.1.0 and older to create rogue WordPress administrator accounts without logging in, giving them full control of vulnerable sites. Site owners should update to WP Maps Pro 6.1.1 immediately and check for unauthorized admin users.

  • Hackers are targeting WordPress websites running WP Maps Pro versions 6.1.0 and older through a critical vulnerability tracked as CVE-2026-8732.
  • The flaw is caused by a temporary access feature whose AJAX endpoint was accessible to unauthenticated users and relied only on a publicly exposed nonce check in frontend JavaScript.
  • Exploitation can create a new WordPress administrator account, generate a passwordless login URL, and automatically authenticate the attacker to the new admin account.
  • Wordfence researchers said they blocked more than 3,600 exploitation attempts in the past 24 hours, indicating the vulnerability is being actively abused.
  • WP Maps Pro version 6.1.1 was released on May 20 to fix CVE-2026-8732, and administrators are urged to update as soon as possible.

Dutch Authorities Dismantle Botnet Linked to 17 Million Infected Devices — Should Read

Dutch authorities disrupted a massive botnet tied to at least 17 million infected devices by seizing servers in the Netherlands, with reports linking the infrastructure to residential proxy service Asocks. The key takeaway is that compromised everyday devices are being abused at huge scale for cybercrime, making basic security hygiene like updates, strong passwords, and trusted apps critical.

  • Dutch authorities announced the takedown of a botnet that had infected at least 17 million devices, including computers, tablets, smartphones, and IoT devices.
  • The Dutch Politie and the National Cyber Security Center said more than 200 servers in the Netherlands served as the botnet’s backend infrastructure.
  • Police seized some of the servers from a hosting provider, and the provider then took the botnet offline after its criminal use was identified.
  • Local outlet NL Times reported that the disrupted service was Asocks, a company that offers residential, corporate, and mobile proxies.
  • The NCSC advised users to reduce botnet risk by keeping systems updated, securing routers and Wi-Fi, using strong passwords and two-factor authentication, changing default passwords, and installing apps only from trusted sources.

item.title: Active Exploitation of PAN-OS CVE-2026-0257 and New Linux CIFSwitch LPE
item.description: The most significant theme in this coverage window is the rapid shift from vulnerability disclosure to observed exploitation, particularly for remote access infrastructure. Reporting centers on an actively exploited Palo Alto Networks GlobalProtect authentication bypass and a newly disclosed Linux...
item.content:encoded:
The most significant theme in this coverage window is the rapid shift from vulnerability disclosure to observed exploitation, particularly for remote access infrastructure. Reporting centers on an actively exploited Palo Alto Networks GlobalProtect authentication bypass and a newly disclosed Linux local privilege escalation flaw that affects multiple mainstream distributions under default configurations. Together, these stories reinforce two persistent operational risks: perimeter devices remain high-value targets for initial access, while local kernel and helper-chain weaknesses continue to offer reliable privilege escalation paths after foothold. The reporting also highlights how implementation trust failures—whether in VPN session handling or kernel-to-userspace request validation—can translate into straightforward attacker abuse. Palo Alto Networks warned that CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass vulnerability affecting PAN-OS and Prisma Access deployments using GlobalProtect, is under active exploitation. The issue applies when GlobalProtect portal or gateway is configured with authentication override cookies enabled and a specific certificate configuration is present, allowing attackers to establish unauthorized VPN sessions on unpatched systems lacking mitigations. Multiple reports point to in-the-wild exploitation rather than purely theoretical risk. Palo Alto described the activity as limited exploit attempts against unpatched devices, while Rapid7 said it observed successful exploitation across numerous customer environments, with the earliest activity beginning on May 17, 2026, and a second wave on May 21. In some cases, attackers reportedly used the flaw to connect through VPN and gain access to internal networks. Technical details indicate the weakness stems from trust in decrypted cookie contents without signature verification. Rapid7 said PAN-OS decrypts authentication override cookies using a configured private key and then accepts the resulting contents as valid, enabling attackers to forge cookies and bypass intended authentication controls. This makes the flaw especially notable because it targets a remote access trust boundary that directly mediates entry into enterprise environments. A separate development is the disclosure of CIFSwitch, a Linux local privilege escalation vulnerability affecting the kernel CIFS subsystem and related userspace handling. The flaw allows an unprivileged attacker to forge CIFS authentication key descriptions and abuse the kernel key request mechanism to reach root-level code execution. According to the researcher, the underlying issue is that the kernel does not verify whether cifs.spnego key requests actually originate from the kernel CIFS client. That gap allows a malicious local user to create a forged request that is then processed by the root-privileged cifs.upcall helper, which trusts attacker-controlled fields. The attack can then force a namespace switch and load a malicious NSS module as root. The researcher reported default-configuration exposure across a broad set of distributions, including Linux Mint 21.3 and 22.3, CentOS Stream 9, Rocky Linux 9, AlmaLinux 9, Kali Linux versions from 2021.4 through 2026.1, and SLES 15 SP7. The breadth of affected platforms makes this disclosure particularly relevant to enterprise Linux fleets and shared multi-user systems, where local privilege escalation remains a critical post-compromise capability.

Palo Alto GlobalProtect VPN auth bypass flaw now exploited in attacks — Must Read

Palo Alto says attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-0257, a PAN-OS GlobalProtect authentication bypass bug that can let them forge auth cookies and gain unauthorized VPN access on unpatched devices. Organizations should patch immediately or disable authentication override/use a separate certificate for it, especially since CISA has added the flaw to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities list.

  • Palo Alto Networks warned that CVE-2026-0257, a PAN-OS GlobalProtect authentication bypass flaw, is now being actively exploited against unpatched devices without mitigations applied.
  • The vulnerability allows attackers to establish unauthorized VPN connections by forging authentication override cookies when GlobalProtect authentication override cookies are enabled and a specific certificate configuration is in place.
  • Rapid7 said it observed exploitation across numerous customers starting on May 17, 2026, and found that some attackers were able to connect to devices via VPN and gain access to internal networks.
  • Rapid7 explained that PAN-OS decrypts authentication override cookies using a configured private key and trusts the decrypted contents without performing any signature verification.
  • Palo Alto Networks advised organizations to install the latest security updates immediately or mitigate the issue by disabling authentication override or using a different certificate for that feature.

New CIFSwitch Linux flaw gives root on multiple distributions — Should Read

A newly disclosed Linux local privilege escalation flaw called CIFSwitch can let unprivileged users gain root by abusing CIFS Kerberos key requests on multiple distributions with vulnerable kernel and cifs-utils combinations. Organizations should patch affected kernels, and if possible mitigate by disabling unused CIFS support, removing cifs-utils, or turning off unprivileged user namespaces.

  • A newly disclosed Linux local privilege escalation vulnerability named CIFSwitch can let attackers forge CIFS authentication key descriptions, abuse the kernel’s key request mechanism, and gain root privileges.
  • The flaw exists because the Linux kernel’s CIFS subsystem does not verify that cifs.spnego key requests originate from the kernel’s CIFS client, allowing an unprivileged user to create a forged request.
  • The root-privileged cifs.upcall helper can trust attacker-controlled fields in the forged request, which can be abused to force a namespace switch and load a malicious NSS module for root code execution.
  • The researcher confirmed default-configuration vulnerability on distributions including Linux Mint 21.3 and 22.3, CentOS Stream 9, Rocky Linux 9, AlmaLinux 9, Kali Linux 2021.4–2026.1, and SLES 15 SP7.
  • CIFSwitch was fixed by a kernel patch that validates cifs.spnego request origins, and the researcher recommends disabling or blacklisting the CIFS module if unused, removing cifs-utils if unnecessary, and disabling unprivileged user namespaces.
